content.problemStatement:
You are given multiple test cases, each describing a binary tree.
For every test case, determine the **size (number of nodes)** of the **largest subtree** that is also a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**.

A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** follows these properties:


The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with values **less than** the node’s value.
The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with values **greater than** the node’s value.
Both the left and right subtrees must themselves be valid BSTs.
Function Declaration

**Function Name**



largestBSTlargestBSTlargestBST



**Parameters**



rootrootroot: A pointer to the root node of the binary tree.



**Return Value**



Returns an integer representing the **size (number of nodes)** of the largest subtree that is a valid **Binary Search Tree (BST)**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the tree. The next line contains N space-separated integers — the node values in level-order traversal of the binary tree (use -1 to represent null or missing nodes).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the size of the largest BST subtree in the given binary tree.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The largest BST subtree is the subtree rooted at node 2, which has a size of 3 (nodes 2, 1, and 3).
The largest BST subtree is the entire tree, which has a size of 7.
The largest BST subtree is the subtree rooted at node 5, which has a size of 1 (only node 5).

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a recursive approach where we check if a subtree is a BST and keep track of the maximum size of the BST subtree found so far. We can use a helper function to check if a tree is a BST by verifying that all nodes in the left subtree are less than the root and all nodes in the right subtree are greater than the root.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the tree, since we visit each node once. O(T*N) total, where T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for the recursive call stack in the worst case when the tree is skewed.
